charts.title: Week 10 Topic Map
charts.description: High-level overview of all major topics covered in Week 10: single-winner voting rules, Condorcet methods, and strategic manipulation.

nodes.label:
Week 10
Voting Theory I
nodes.note: Voting theory studies how groups of agents aggregate individual preferences into a single collective decision. This week covers single-winner rules, Condorcet consistency, and the Gibbard-Satterthwaite impossibility theorem on strategic manipulation.

nodes.label:
Introduction
to Voting
nodes.note: Computational social choice sits at the intersection of algorithms, game theory, and economics. It analyses how voting rules aggregate preferences and explores paradoxes and impossibility results that arise in collective decision making.

nodes.label:
Positional Scoring
Rules
nodes.note: Positional scoring rules assign points to candidates based on their rank in each voter's ordering. The total score across all voters determines the winner. Examples include Plurality, Borda, Veto, and k-approval.

nodes.label:
Elimination
Methods
nodes.note: Elimination-based methods iteratively remove candidates from contention. Instant-Runoff Voting (IRV) removes the candidate with fewest first-place votes each round until one candidate remains.

nodes.label:
Condorcet
Principle
nodes.note: The Condorcet criterion requires electing a candidate who beats every other candidate in pairwise majority comparisons, if such a candidate exists. No positional scoring rule satisfies this for three or more candidates.

nodes.label:
Strategic
Manipulation
nodes.note: A voting rule is strategyproof if no voter can benefit by misreporting their true preferences. The Gibbard-Satterthwaite theorem shows that any non-dictatorial onto voting rule with three or more candidates is manipulable.

nodes.label: Plurality
nodes.note: Plurality uses scoring vector (1, 0, ..., 0): each voter gives one point to their top-ranked candidate and zero to all others. Simple to compute but can elect candidates opposed by a majority.

nodes.label: Borda Count
nodes.note: Borda count uses scoring vector (m-1, m-2, ..., 0): a candidate ranked first gets m-1 points, second gets m-2, and so on. It rewards overall popularity across rankings.

nodes.label: IRV
nodes.note: Instant-Runoff Voting repeatedly eliminates the candidate with fewest first-place votes, redistributing those votes to each ballot's next preference. Used in Ireland, Australia, and US ranked-choice elections.

nodes.label: Condorcet Winner
nodes.note: A Condorcet winner beats every other candidate in head-to-head majority comparisons. It may not exist (Condorcet paradox: preferences can cycle). When one exists, a Condorcet-consistent rule must elect it.

nodes.label:
Copeland
Rule
nodes.note: Copeland scores each candidate by pairwise wins (+1) and ties (+0.5). The Condorcet winner, if it exists, scores m-1 and is always elected, making Copeland Condorcet-consistent.

nodes.label: Maximin Rule
nodes.note: Maximin elects the candidate whose worst-case pairwise support is highest. The Condorcet winner's minimum pairwise support exceeds n/2, guaranteeing Condorcet consistency.

nodes.label: Dodgson Rule
nodes.note: Dodgson scores a candidate by the minimum number of adjacent swaps in voter rankings needed to make them a Condorcet winner. The Condorcet winner scores zero. Computing Dodgson scores is NP-hard.

nodes.label:
Gibbard-
Satterthwaite
nodes.note: The Gibbard-Satterthwaite theorem states that for three or more candidates, any resolute voting rule that is onto (every candidate can win) and strategyproof must be a dictatorship. This means all reasonable rules are manipulable.

nodes.label:
Single-Peaked
Preferences
nodes.note: Single-peaked preferences lie along a linear order: each voter has a peak, and their utility decreases monotonically in both directions away from that peak. Under single-peaked preferences, the median-voter rule is strategyproof.

charts.title: Instant-Runoff Voting (IRV) Algorithm
charts.description: Step-by-step procedure for determining the winner under Instant-Runoff Voting (also known as STV with k=1).

nodes.label:
Start: n voters,
m candidates
nodes.note: We are given a set of voter rankings over m candidates and want to find the IRV winner.

nodes.label:
Initialise: all
candidates active
nodes.note: Mark all m candidates as active. Each voter's current top preference is their highest-ranked candidate among active candidates.

nodes.label:
Count first-place
votes per candidate
nodes.note: For each active candidate, count how many voters currently rank them first (among active candidates only).

nodes.label:
Any candidate
has majority?
nodes.note: Check if any candidate has strictly more than n/2 first-place votes. A majority ensures a clear winner. For two remaining candidates, one always has a majority.

nodes.label: WINNER ELECTED
nodes.note: The candidate with a majority of first-place votes is elected as the IRV winner.

nodes.label:
Find candidate
with fewest votes
nodes.note: Identify the active candidate with the fewest first-place votes. Use alphabetical or defined tie-breaking if multiple candidates are tied for last place.

nodes.label:
Eliminate that
candidate
nodes.note: Remove the weakest candidate from the election. All ballots that had this candidate ranked first now promote the next active candidate on their ballot.

nodes.label:
Only 1 candidate
remains?
nodes.note: If only one candidate is still active, they win by default. In practice this case is covered by the majority check, but this guards against edge cases.

nodes.label:
LAST CANDIDATE
WINS
nodes.note: The final remaining active candidate is elected as the IRV winner.

charts.title: Finding the Condorcet Winner
charts.description: Algorithm to determine whether a Condorcet winner exists in a given preference profile and identify it if so.

nodes.label:
Start: preference
profile R, candidates C
nodes.note: We have a set of voter rankings over candidates and want to know if any candidate beats all others in pairwise majority comparisons.

nodes.label:
Pick candidate c
as Condorcet candidate
nodes.note: Start with the first candidate as a potential Condorcet winner. We will test it against all others.

nodes.label:
Any untested
opponent d remains?
nodes.note: Check whether there is still an opponent we have not compared c against in a head-to-head majority vote.

nodes.label:
Count voters
preferring c over d
nodes.note: For each voter, check whether c is ranked above d. Count how many voters prefer c to d and how many prefer d to c.

nodes.label:
Majority prefer
c over d?
nodes.note: If more than half the voters prefer c to d, then c wins the pairwise comparison. If not, c fails and cannot be the Condorcet winner.

nodes.label:
Mark d as tested,
continue
nodes.note: c has beaten d. Move on to compare c against the next untested opponent.

nodes.label:
c eliminated:
next candidate
nodes.note: c has lost to d and cannot be the Condorcet winner. Move to the next untested candidate and start over.

nodes.label:
Untested candidates
remain?
nodes.note: Check if there are any candidates we have not yet tried as a potential Condorcet winner.

nodes.label:
CONDORCET WINNER
FOUND: c
nodes.note: Candidate c has beaten every other candidate in pairwise majority comparisons and is the unique Condorcet winner of this profile.

nodes.label:
NO CONDORCET
WINNER EXISTS
nodes.note: All candidates have been eliminated. There is no Condorcet winner — the profile likely contains a Condorcet cycle (majority preferences cycle: a beats b beats c beats a).

charts.title: Voting Rule Classification
charts.description: Taxonomy of single-winner voting rules by their type, properties, and Condorcet consistency.

nodes.label:
Single-Winner
Voting Rules
nodes.note: Single-winner voting rules take a preference profile and return one or more candidates as winners. They differ in what information they use and what axiomatic properties they satisfy.

nodes.label:
Positional Scoring
Rules (PSR)
nodes.note: Positional scoring rules assign scores to candidates based solely on their position in each voter's ranking. They are defined by a scoring vector s = (s1, ..., sm) with s1 >= ... >= sm and s1 > sm.

nodes.label:
Elimination-Based
Methods
nodes.note: Elimination-based methods iteratively remove candidates in rounds until a winner emerges. They use more of the preference information than PSRs but are computationally more complex.

nodes.label:
Condorcet-Consistent
Methods
nodes.note: Condorcet-consistent methods are defined to elect the Condorcet winner whenever one exists. They use pairwise majority information rather than positional scoring.

nodes.label:
Plurality
(1,0,...,0)
nodes.note: Plurality gives one point to each voter's top-ranked candidate. It is the simplest PSR but can elect candidates opposed by the majority when there are three or more candidates.

nodes.label:
Borda Count
(m-1,...,1,0)
nodes.note: Borda count sums positional scores across all voters. It rewards broadly popular candidates but is susceptible to strategic manipulation by burying opponents.

nodes.label:
Veto
(1,...,1,0)
nodes.note: Veto scoring gives each candidate one point from every voter except the one who ranked them last. Equivalent to penalising last-place rankings rather than rewarding top ones.

nodes.label: IRV / STV
nodes.note: Instant-Runoff Voting repeatedly eliminates the candidate with fewest first-place votes. Used in many real elections but can fail to elect the Condorcet winner.

nodes.label:
Copeland Rule
(pairwise wins)
nodes.note: Copeland scores candidates by the number of pairwise majority wins. It is Condorcet-consistent and easy to compute but can produce ties.

nodes.label:
Maximin Rule
(best worst-case)
nodes.note: Maximin elects the candidate whose worst pairwise support is highest. It is Condorcet-consistent and avoids candidates who are strongly disliked by some group.

nodes.label:
Dodgson Rule
(min swaps)
nodes.note: Dodgson elects the candidate closest to being a Condorcet winner, measured by the minimum number of adjacent ballot swaps needed. Computing this is NP-hard.
